JavaScript Core (35) - Functions and How This Works - What Is a Function?
New chapter time — and this one comes with the notorious
Before we get into this chapter, I'd recommend keeping one idea in the back of your head: JavaScript is basically made up of objects, all the way down. Why do I say that? Back in "
JavaScript Core (24) - Objects - Objects and Primitives (article in Mandarin)
" we confirmed something — we can actually attach a property to a function too.
So let's take another look at what a function structure can look like.
First off, a function is defined and declared using the
keyword paired with a name. On top of that, a function can take in parameters and has its own block of code plus its own scope — in other words, its own execution context.
Beyond that, a function has the ability to be called —
. And there's one more feature worth calling out, one I haven't mentioned yet: it has its own
Let me quickly round up what makes a function special:
It has its own block of code
It has its own execution context
A name (optional — that's the difference between anonymous and named functions)
The simplest way to tell an expression from a statement is to look at whether it returns something. We've already written the function declaration form earlier:
The thing about a function declaration is that it doesn't hand you a result on its own — you have to call it before it gives you anything. So what about expressions?
A function expression returns the function into
while your code runs. This is the same idea we covered back in
JavaScript Core (11) - Operators, Types and Grammar - Statements and Expressions (article in Mandarin)
. This particular form is also called an anonymous expression — a function with no name. But heads up: not every function expression is nameless.
Here's an example of a function expression that
This named function expression has a quirk: the function's own name can only be called from inside itself, not from the outside.
So when do you use anonymous vs named functions? Honestly this one comes down a lot to your team's habits. If you go by the Airbnb

, they'd rather you avoid function declarations where you can. The main reason is that JavaScript hoisting can make debugging a headache. There's another camp that says as long as you've got a solid grip on hoisting, using named or anonymous functions is totally fine either way. So using a function expression really can cut down on debugging pain — in real-world dev, "hoisting" can sometimes force you to mentally trace the whole flow before you can pin down where the problem is. But if you still want to use a function declaration, that's fine too — just make sure you declare it before you call it, so the function definitely exists by then.
